# Autogenerated AGENTS.md Files Can Hurt Coding Agent Performance

## Lightning Takeaways
- Autogenerated context files can decrease coding-agent task success while increasing cost.
- More instructions did not mean faster fixing; agents often took more steps and explored more.
- Human-written context files showed only small gains in most documented repos.
- Context files looked most useful when repos had little or no documentation.

## Summary
This video breaks down benchmark results on repository context files for coding agents (`AGENTS.md` style files). You’ll get a practical decision framework for when to skip them, when to keep them minimal, and when they can still be useful.

![Video thumbnail](https://i.ytimg.com/vi/s95ISC-KeIM/hqdefault.jpg)

## Key Insights

1) **The “free optimization” assumption failed in testing.**  
Claim: Autogenerated context files underperformed.  
Evidence: The transcript reports lower success (~3% drop) with higher inference cost (20%+ increase).  
Implication: Auto-generating context can make your pipeline both worse and more expensive.

2) **Human-written files were only a marginal improvement.**  
Claim: Developer-authored files were not a major breakthrough.  
Evidence: Reported gains were small (~4%) and still incurred cost overhead.  
Implication: File quality and scope matter more than just “having a file.”

3) **Agents used the files, then over-explored.**  
Claim: The issue was not ignoring instructions; it was over-compliance.  
Evidence: More tool usage, broader exploration, more tests, and extra steps were observed.  
Implication: Long context files can create analysis paralysis.

4) **Context files can help in a documentation vacuum.**  
Claim: There is still a legitimate use case.  
Evidence: In documentation-removed conditions, generated context outperformed no-file conditions.  
Implication: Context files should be fallback tools, not default policy.

## What Most Teams Miss
This is not a universal “never use context files” result. It is mostly a placement/scope problem: context helps when information is missing, and hurts when it duplicates or overloads.

## Caveat
This blueprint reflects coding-agent benchmark contexts in the transcript, not every language/runtime scenario.

## One Action to Take
Run one A/B test this week: same task set, with and without autogenerated context files, and compare success rate + token cost.
